name: AWS Amplify Hosting
description: Amazon's fully managed hosting service for static and server-rendered web apps with Git-based CI/CD, powered by CloudFront CDN and integrated with the broader AWS ecosystem.

description.value: Amazon's fully managed hosting service for static and server-rendered web apps with Git-based CI/CD, powered by CloudFront CDN and integrated with the broader AWS ecosystem.

year-launched.comment: AWS Amplify Console (now Amplify Hosting) launched on November 26, 2018. The broader Amplify framework launched in 2017.

open-source.comment: The hosting platform itself is a proprietary AWS managed service. The Amplify CLI, JS libraries, and framework adapters are open source, but the core hosting infrastructure is not.

primary-focus.comment: Amplify Hosting supports both static/JAMstack sites and SSR full-stack applications with serverless backend integration.

free-tier.comment: 12-month free tier for new AWS customers. Pay-as-you-go after that with no minimum fees, so very low traffic sites may still cost effectively $0.

free-tier-bandwidth.comment: 15 GB served per month for 12 months for new AWS customers.

free-tier-build-minutes.comment: 1,000 build minutes per month for 12 months for new AWS customers.

free-tier-sites.comment: Default quota is 25 apps per region per account. New accounts may start with a lower quota that increases based on usage. No separate site limit on the free tier.

paid-plan-starting-price.comment: No fixed monthly plans. After free tier: $0.01/build minute, $0.023/GB storage/month, $0.15/GB served. Costs scale with usage.

bandwidth-overage-cost.comment: After the 12-month free tier expires, all data transfer is charged at $0.15/GB served.

enterprise-pricing.comment: AWS offers Enterprise Support plans and custom pricing through AWS Organizations and Enterprise Discount Programs.

git-integration.comment: Supports GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and AWS CodeCommit. CodeCommit is not listed as a tag option but is supported.

auto-deploy.comment: Pushes to connected branches automatically trigger builds and deployments.

cli-deployment.comment: Supports manual deployments via the Amplify CLI (amplify publish) and AWS CLI (create-deployment / start-deployment). Also supports drag-and-drop uploads without a Git repo.

build-system.comment: Built-in CI/CD build pipeline with configurable build settings via amplify.yml.

supported-frameworks.comment: First-party SSR support for Next.js (zero-config), Nuxt (preset adapter). Community adapters for Astro, SvelteKit. Static support for Gatsby, Hugo, Jekyll, Eleventy, VuePress. Also supports React, Angular, Vue, Ionic, Ember as SPAs. Vite-based projects work as SPAs.

monorepo-support.comment: Supports npm workspaces, pnpm workspaces, Yarn workspaces, Nx, and Turborepo monorepos with automatic build tool detection.

deploy-previews.comment: Web previews deploy every pull request to a unique preview URL. For GitHub repos, the Amplify bot comments the preview URL on the PR.

instant-rollbacks.comment: Amplify has a 'Redeploy this version' button that triggers a rebuild from a previous commit, but it is not an instant rollback (requires going through the full build/deploy process, taking 5-10 minutes). True instant rollback is not available.

deploy-notifications.comment: Built-in email notifications via Amazon SNS. Webhook support for incoming webhooks. Slack notifications require manual setup via SNS + Lambda. GitHub status checks are posted via the Amplify GitHub App.

global-cdn.comment: Assets are served from the Amazon CloudFront global CDN.

edge-locations.comment: CloudFront operates 600+ Points of Presence in 100+ cities across 50+ countries. The exact number available to Amplify Hosting is the full CloudFront network.

http2.comment: CloudFront serves content over HTTP/2 by default, even if the origin doesn't support it.

http3.comment: CloudFront supports HTTP/3, but Amplify Hosting uses a managed CloudFront distribution where HTTP/3 is not automatically enabled and cannot be configured by the user. There is an open feature request for this.

response-headers.comment: Custom HTTP response headers can be configured via the Amplify console or a customHttp.yml file in the project root.

image-optimization.comment: Built-in image optimization for SSR (WEB_COMPUTE) apps, with CDN caching of optimized images. Available as an integration point for framework authors.

asset-compression.comment: Both gzip and Brotli compression are enabled by default on Amplify Hosting apps via CloudFront managed cache policies.

custom-domains.comment: Custom domains can be connected via Amazon Route 53 or third-party DNS providers.

free-ssl.comment: Amplify automatically provisions free SSL/TLS certificates via AWS Certificate Manager (ACM). Certificates are valid for 13 months and renew automatically.

wildcard-ssl.comment: Wildcard SSL certificates are supported for wildcard subdomains (*.example.com).

auto-https-redirect.comment: All traffic is secured through HTTPS by default. HTTP requests are automatically redirected to HTTPS.

custom-domain-preview.comment: Preview deployments can use custom subdomains when using Amazon Route 53 for DNS. Automatic subdomain creation assigns subdomains like pr-123.example.com to preview deployments. Only available with Route 53 managed domains.

serverless-functions.comment: Amplify Functions are powered by AWS Lambda and can be defined as part of the Amplify project. Supports both API-triggered and event-driven functions.

functions-runtime.comment: Primary development experience is TypeScript/Node.js. Supports any AWS Lambda runtime including Python, Go, Ruby, Java, .NET, and custom runtimes. Amplify Gen 2 focuses on TypeScript but allows configuring alternative runtimes.

edge-functions.comment: Amplify uses Lambda@Edge behind the scenes for SSR applications. Edge functions run at CloudFront edge locations for low-latency responses.

scheduled-functions.comment: Functions can be scheduled using natural language or cron expressions, powered by Amazon EventBridge Rules.

form-handling.comment: No built-in form handling. Form submissions require custom backend implementation using Lambda functions, API Gateway, or third-party services.

authentication.comment: Built-in authentication via Amazon Cognito, with pre-built UI components (Authenticator) for React, Vue, Angular, and more. Supports email/password, social sign-in, MFA, and OIDC/SAML providers.

kv-storage.comment: No built-in key-value or edge storage solution within Amplify Hosting. S3 and DynamoDB can be integrated but are separate AWS services, not a first-party KV store in the hosting platform.

database-integration.comment: First-party database integration via Amplify Data (powered by AWS AppSync + DynamoDB). Also supports connecting to external DynamoDB tables and SQL databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL).

redirects.comment: Supports 301, 302, and other redirect types. Configurable via the Amplify console or JSON configuration.

rewrites.comment: 200 rewrites are supported, allowing content from a different path to be served without changing the URL. Configured alongside redirects.

custom-404.comment: Custom 404 pages can be configured via redirect/rewrite rules.

spa-fallback.comment: SPA fallback routing is supported via a 200 rewrite rule that serves index.html for all paths except static file extensions.

headers-config.comment: Custom response headers can be defined globally or per-path via the Amplify console or customHttp.yml file.

ab-testing.comment: No native A/B testing or traffic splitting feature. Users can manually achieve similar results using branch-based deployments with different subdomains, but automatic traffic splitting is not available. Open feature request since 2019.

free-team-members.comment: Team access is managed through AWS IAM users and roles, not a per-seat model. No inherent limit on team members, but access is controlled by the AWS account's IAM policies.

rbac.comment: Fine-grained access control via AWS IAM policies. AWS provides managed policies like AdministratorAccess-Amplify and custom policies can be created for granular permissions.

audit-logs.comment: AWS CloudTrail provides audit logging for all Amplify API calls. CloudWatch Logs captures build and SSR runtime logs.

environment-variables.comment: Environment variables can be configured per-app or per-branch. All variables are encrypted at rest.

preview-comments.comment: No built-in preview commenting or annotation feature. The Amplify GitHub bot posts the preview URL as a PR comment, but there is no on-page annotation capability. This is an open feature request.

ddos-protection.comment: Basic DDoS protection is provided via CloudFront (AWS Shield Standard is included at no additional cost). Enhanced protection available through AWS WAF integration.

waf.comment: AWS WAF integration available with one-click setup in the Amplify console. Adds $15/month per app plus standard AWS WAF pricing. Includes managed rules for SQL injection, XSS, and bot protection.

soc2.comment: AWS as a whole holds SOC 2 Type II certification. AWS Amplify runs on SOC 2 certified infrastructure.

gdpr.comment: AWS provides GDPR-compliant infrastructure with a Data Processing Addendum (DPA) in AWS Service Terms. All AWS services can be used in compliance with GDPR.

password-protection.comment: Branch-level password protection via username/password basic auth. Can be applied globally or per-branch. Amplify throttles failed login attempts to protect against dictionary attacks.

ip-access-control.comment: IP-based access control is available through AWS WAF integration (not built-in natively). WAF allows creating rules to allow or block requests from specified IP ranges. Requires WAF addon ($15/month + WAF costs).

documentation-quality.comment: Comprehensive documentation split between AWS Amplify Hosting docs and Amplify Gen 2 docs. Good coverage of features, examples, and troubleshooting. The split between Gen 1 and Gen 2 documentation can be confusing. Searchable and regularly updated.

integrations.comment: Integrates with AWS ecosystem: Amazon Pinpoint (analytics), Amazon Cognito (auth), DynamoDB/AppSync (database), CloudWatch (monitoring). No native CMS, commerce, or search integrations as first-party features.

community.comment: Active Discord community (~20,000 members) with an official 'Hey, Amplify!' bot. GitHub Discussions on the amplify-docs repo. Questions tagged 'aws-amplify' on Stack Overflow. AWS re:Post also available for support.

status-page.comment: AWS Health Dashboard provides public service health status for all AWS services including Amplify at health.aws.amazon.com. No Amplify-specific status page.
